title: Copper Supply-Demand Cycle
core_question: Is copper a structural supply-demand shortage theme or a cyclical macro trade?
thesis: Copper should be analyzed as both a structural electrification constraint and a macro-sensitive commodity. The structural thesis depends on long mine lead times, declining ore grades, grid and data-center demand, and limited rapid supply response; the cyclical risk is that weak construction or manufacturing demand offsets electrification growth.
mechanism: Copper demand rises with electrification, grid expansion, data centers, EVs, renewables, and industrial activity, while mine supply responds slowly because permitting, development, ore grade, water, power, and jurisdictional risk constrain new output.
drivers: Grid expansion and electrification increase copper intensity.
Data-center and AI power infrastructure add incremental electrical demand.
Mine development lead times are long and ore grades are under pressure.
Macro cycles still drive short-term demand through property, construction, and manufacturing.

bottlenecks.name: mine supply response and quality reserve scarcity
bottlenecks.technical_reason: New copper supply requires multi-year mine development, permitting, infrastructure, water and power access, and attractive ore grades.

segments.name: low-cost copper miners
segments.role: Produce copper with reserve quality, scale, and operating leverage to price.

segments.name: smelters and refiners
segments.role: Convert concentrate into refined copper, with economics tied to treatment charges and supply balance.

segments.name: electrical equipment and grid users
segments.role: Consume copper in cables, transformers, motors, data centers, EVs, and renewable infrastructure.

profit_pools.name: scarce low-cost reserves
profit_pools.rationale: If price rises faster than costs, miners with quality reserves and operating leverage capture the largest profit pool.

profit_pools.name: recycling and substitution pressure
profit_pools.rationale: High prices can increase recycling economics and substitution attempts, limiting upside duration.

scenarios.name: bull
scenarios.description: Electrification and grid demand exceed supply additions while inventories stay tight.

scenarios.name: bear
scenarios.description: Macro demand weakens or substitution/recycling offsets structural growth.
implications: copper behaves more like a cyclical commodity

evidence.title: Major copper miner disclosures and reserve reports
claims: Reserve quality, cost position, and project pipeline determine mining exposure to copper price.

evidence.title: Industry copper supply-demand research
claims: Electrification demand and long mine lead times can create structural tightness.

counter_theses: China property and global manufacturing weakness could offset electrification demand.
High prices could accelerate substitution, thrift, and recycling.
New projects or disrupted mines returning to production could loosen supply.
tracking_signals: Mine disruption and project approval news.
China property, grid, and manufacturing indicators.
